rawtext.text:
Shh — no, come in, come in. Mind the rubbings; the charcoal smears.

I am Maelis Vane, keeper of what little Elderfall remembers. Forty years I have copied the carvings on these ruins, and in forty years they have never once changed. Until the night before you arrived. §oThree glyphs rewrote themselves.§r I watched it happen. Do you understand what that means? The stones are §oawake§r, and they are writing about §byou§r.

The oldest glyphs tell of the §5Hollow King§r and his bargain — you have heard that tale from Aldric, no doubt, and he tells it well enough for a man who cannot read a single rune.

But the newer carvings, the ones that changed... they read: §o"The seal is a candle. The candle gutters. The Awoken is the hand that shields the flame — or the wind that kills it."§r

A warning, or a threat. The grammar of dead languages is regrettably ambiguous.

The first Awoken has no name in the record — deliberately, I think. Names are doors, and the carvers were closing doors.

What the stones do say: the Awoken did not defeat the Hollow King with strength. They §oout-remembered§r him. They carried the memory of every villager, every field, every small kindness into the shrine, and the Hollow King — who had traded all his memories for that rusting crown — could not stand against it.

So when the villagers here treat you kindly, accept it. Their regard is not sentiment. §bIt is armament.§r

My knees are older than my curiosity is patient. There are three ruin fragments I can no longer walk to:

§6Echoes Beneath§r — visit the §fSunken Archway§r, the §fShattered Bell Tower§r, and the §fMoss-bound Obelisk§r around the village. Stand before each and simply §olisten§r. Whatever you feel there, I want it in your own words. The stones speak louder to you than they ever have to me.
